1: wayyāqom ʾelyāšîḇ hakkōhēn haggāḏôl wᵉʾeḥāyw hakkōhănîm wayyiḇnû ʾet-šaʿar haṣṣōʾn: 'and Eliashib the high priest arose with his fellow priests and they built the Sheep Gate.' The chapter's governing verb is ḥāzaq (strengthen, repair, fortify) — used 35 times in this chapter as the technical term for wall repair. qiḏᵉšûhû: 'they dedicated/consecrated it' — the Sheep Gate uniquely receives the verb qāḏaš (to set apart as holy), appropriate since the high priest and priests built it and it was the entry point for sacrificial animals going to the temple. The consecration also applies to the towers flanking it. miggaddal hamêʾāh (Tower of the Hundred) and miggaddal ḥananʾēl (Tower of Hananel) are probably the northeastern corner towers of the city.
2: wᵉʿal-yāḏô bānû ʾanšê yᵉrîḥô: 'and next to him the men of Jericho built.' The phrase ʿal-yāḏô (literally 'at his hand' = next to him) is the standard construction formula for the chapter, indicating each section's adjacency to the previous worker. The men of Jericho represent the broader district workers from outside Jerusalem proper who contributed to the project — the wall-building was a national Judean effort, not merely a local Jerusalem project. waʿal-yāḏô ḥāzaq zakkûr ben-ʾimmrî: Zaccur son of Imri, a Levite, built the next section.
3: wᵉʾet-šaʿar haddāgîm bānû bᵉnê hassᵉnāʾāh: 'and the sons of Hassenaah built the Fish Gate.' šaʿar haddāgîm (Fish Gate) was located on the northern wall where the fish market operated (cf. Zeph 1:10). The construction detail is precise: hēm qērûhû wayyaʿămîḏû daltōtāyw manʿûlāyw ûbᵉrîḥāyw, 'they laid its beams and set its doors, its locks, and its bars.' The four components — qôrôt (beams), daltôt (doors/leaves), manʿûlîm (bolts, from nāʿal to fasten), bᵉrîḥîm (bars, cross-bars) — constitute the full security apparatus of a city gate.
4: wᵉʿal-yāḏām ḥāzaq mᵉrēmôt ben-ʾûriyyāh ben-haqqôṣ: 'and next to them Meremoth son of Uriah son of Hakkoz repaired.' Meremoth appears again in v.21, building a second section — unusual zeal. He also appears in Ezra 8:33 receiving the temple treasures Ezra brought from Babylon. The Ben-Hakkoz family appears in Ezra 2:61 among the priests who could not prove their genealogy (and were therefore barred from the priesthood) — yet Meremoth served faithfully. waʿal-yāḏô ḥāzaq mešullām ben-bᵉreḵyāh ben-mešêzaḇʾēl: Meshullam son of Berechiah, who also built a second section (v.30). His daughter married Tobiah's son (Neh 6:18), creating an irony: a man who built the wall had family ties to the primary opponent.
5: waʿal-yāḏām ḥāzaq hattᵉqôʿîm wᵉʾaddîrêhem lōʾ-hēḇîʾû ṣawwᵉʾrām bᵉʿăḇōḏat ʾăḏōnêhem: 'and next to them the Tekoites repaired, but their nobles did not bring their neck to the work of their Lord.' This is the one negative note in the wall-building record. ʾaddîrîm (nobles, great ones) refused to participate; the metaphor is vivid: they would not 'bring their neck' (lōʾ-hēḇîʾû ṣawwᵉʾrām), i.e., bend to the yoke of labor. Yet the Tekoite common workers built not only their assigned section here (v.5) but also a second section (v.27) — the ordinary citizens compensated for the nobles' refusal. Tekoa was 12 miles south of Jerusalem, a pastoral town (Amos was from Tekoa); its distance made the workers' participation more remarkable.
6: wᵉʾet-šaʿar hayšānāh ḥizzᵉqû yôyāḏāʿ ben-pāsēaḥ ûmešullām ben-bᵉsôḏyāh: 'and the Old Gate was repaired by Joiada son of Paseah and Meshullam son of Besodeiah.' šaʿar hayšānāh (Gate of the Old/Jeshanah) is obscure — some identify it with the Mishneh (Second Quarter) gate; the Hebrew yāšān means old. The construction details: hēm ‌ṭāḥᵉnûhû wayyaʿămîḏû daltōtāyw manʿûlāyw ûḇrîḥāyw — same formula as v.3, indicating a standardized construction record, likely drawn from an official list maintained during the project.
7: waʿal-yāḏām ḥāzaq mᵉlaṭyāh haggiḇʿônî wᵉyāḏôn hamêrônōṯî ʾanšê giḇʿôn ûhammiṣpāh: 'and next to them Melatiah the Gibeonite and Jadon the Meronothite repaired, the men of Gibeon and Mizpah.' Gibeon and Mizpah are northwest of Jerusalem, in Benjamin territory. The phrase that follows is textually difficult: lᵉkissēʾ paḥat ʿēḇer hannāhār, 'belonging to the throne/seat of the governor of Beyond the River' — possibly indicating these towns fell under the direct jurisdiction of the Persian province rather than the local Jerusalem administration. The inclusion of Benjaminite towns reflects the ethnic-geographic breadth of the project.
8: ʿal-yāḏô ḥāzaq ʿuzzîʾēl ben-ḥarhayāh ṣōrᵉp̄îm: 'next to him Uzziel son of Harhaiah, goldsmiths (ṣōrᵉp̄îm).' The mention of professional guilds — goldsmiths (ṣōrᵉp̄îm, from ṣārap̄ to refine/smelt) and perfumers (rōqᵉḥîm, from rāqaḥ to mix/compound) — indicates that the artisan guilds of Jerusalem participated in the wall project alongside priests, officials, and farmers. wayyaʿăzᵉḇû yᵉrûšālaim ʿad-haḥômāh hārᵉḥāḇāh: 'and they restored Jerusalem as far as the Broad Wall.' The Broad Wall (haḥômāh hārᵉḥāḇāh) was a massive 8th-century BCE fortification on the northwest; archaeological excavations have confirmed its existence at approximately 7 meters wide.
9: wᵉʿal-yāḏām ḥāzaq rᵉp̄āyāh ben-ḥûr śar ḥᵉṣî peleḵ yᵉrûšālaim: 'and next to them Rephaiah son of Hur, ruler of half the district of Jerusalem.' śar ḥᵉṣî peleḵ: 'ruler of half the district' — peleḵ is a Persian administrative term (possibly from Akkadian) for a sub-district. Jerusalem's district was divided into two halves, each with its own ruler (cf. v.12: Shallum, ruler of the other half). The dual administration reflects Persian provincial organization at the local level.
10: waʿal-yāḏô ḥāzaq yᵉḏāʿyāh ben-ḥărûmap̄ wᵉnegeḏ bêtô: 'and next to him Jedaiah son of Harumaph repaired, and opposite his own house.' The phrase negeḏ bêtô (opposite his own house) is the chapter's most humanizing detail — individuals and families built the section of wall adjacent to their own homes. This had obvious practical motivation (you protect your own door first) but also created personal ownership of the project: each homeowner's section was their responsibility. Ḥattûš ben-ḥašabnᵉyāh built next to him.
11: ḥēzeq šēnî ḥāzaq malkiyyāh ben-ḥārîm wᵉḥaššûb ben-paḥat-môʾāḇ: 'Malchijah son of Harim and Hasshub son of Pahath-moab repaired a second section.' ḥēzeq šēnî is unique — only here in the chapter does a worker build a 'second section' (not a separate second assignment, but a stretch adjoining the first). The Tower of the Ovens (migdal hatannûrîm) was likely a tower named for the bakers' quarter nearby; tannûr is the clay oven used for baking bread, still used as the standard oven type (tannur) in the Middle East.
12: waʿal-yāḏô ḥāzaq šallûm ben-hallôḥēš śar ḥᵉṣî peleḵ yᵉrûšālaim hûʾ ûḇᵉnôtāyw: 'and next to him Shallum son of Hallohesh, ruler of the other half-district of Jerusalem, he and his daughters.' ûḇᵉnôtāyw (and his daughters) is the chapter's most remarkable detail — women explicitly participating in the heavy labor of wall construction. No other text in the OT records women in physical construction work. The daughters are unnamed but their contribution is permanently recorded. This detail may also explain the intense personal motivation: when the family unit builds together on the section protecting their own home, the work transcends the administrative and becomes familial devotion.
13: ʾet-šaʿar haggay ḥizzᵉqû ḥānûn wᵉyōšᵉḇê zānôaḥ: 'the Valley Gate was repaired by Hanun and the inhabitants of Zanoah.' Hanun is possibly the same official named in Neh 2:13 as the starting point of Nehemiah's nocturnal inspection. Zanoah was a village in the Shephelah region. The section length is given: ʾelep̄ ammāh, 'a thousand cubits' (approximately 500 meters) — the longest assigned section in the chapter, suggesting either Zanoah provided exceptional manpower or this stretch was less damaged. ʿad šaʿar hāʾašpōt: up to the Dung Gate, establishing the southern wall assignment.
14: wᵉʾet-šaʿar hāʾašpôt ḥizzᵉq malkiyyāh ben-rēḵāb śar peleḵ bêt-hakkārem: 'and the Dung Gate was repaired by Malchijah son of Rechab, ruler of the district of Beth-haccherem.' Beth-haccherem is possibly identified with modern Ramat Rachel, a site south of Jerusalem. Malchijah is a district ruler — the Persian administrative structure of Judah (the medinah) included several sub-districts, each contributing labor proportional to their administrative unit. The Dung Gate was the lowest-prestige gate of the city (located near the rubbish dumps); its assignment to a district ruler rather than a local figure may reflect the organizational pragmatics of the project.
15: wᵉʾet-šaʿar hāʿayin ḥizzᵉq šallûn ben-kôl-ḥōzeʾ śar peleḵ hammiṣpāh: 'and the Fountain Gate was repaired by Shallun son of Col-hozeh, ruler of the district of Mizpah.' The Fountain Gate (šaʿar hāʿayin) opened near the Gihon spring, Jerusalem's primary water source. hûʾ yiḇnennû wᵉyiṣōpennû: 'he built it and roofed it' — the Fountain Gate received special treatment beyond other gates, with a roof added. wᵉdaltōtāyw manʿûlāyw ûḇᵉrîḥāyw — the standard gate hardware. The Pool of Shelah (bᵉrêḵat haššelaḥ) and the king's garden are mentioned as landmarks; Šelaḥ (= Siloam) connects to Hezekiah's tunnel system.
16: ʾaḥărāyw ḥāzaq nᵉḥemyāh ben-ʿazḇûq śar ḥᵉṣî peleḵ bêt-ṣûr: 'after him Nehemiah son of Azbuk, ruler of half the district of Beth-zur.' This Nehemiah is not the author of the book — the memoir author carefully uses first-person (ʾanî) when referring to himself. Beth-zur was an important city in Judah (about 25 km south of Jerusalem). This Nehemiah built ʿad-negeḏ qiḇrê ḏāwîḏ (opposite the tombs of David) — the royal tombs on the eastern slope of the City of David are here confirmed as still identifiable landmarks in the 5th century BCE.
17: ʾaḥărāyw ḥāzᵉqû halwiyyim rᵉḥûm ben-bānî: 'after him the Levites repaired — Rehum son of Bani.' The Levitical participation in wall-building alongside their liturgical duties reflects the holistic community organization Nehemiah achieved. ʾal-yāḏô ḥāzaq ḥašabyāh śar ḥᵉṣî peleḵ qᵉʿîlāh: 'next to him Hashabiah, ruler of half the district of Keilah' — Keilah is the city that David once defended (1 Sam 23). Its ruler's participation in Jerusalem's wall shows the provincial solidarity of the Judean districts with the capital.
18: ʾaḥărāyw ḥāzᵉqû ʾăḥêhem bawway ben-hênaḏāḏ śar ḥᵉṣî peleḵ qᵉʿîlāh: 'after them their brothers repaired: Binnui son of Henadad, ruler of the other half-district of Keilah.' The Henadad family appears in Ezra 3:9 as Levites who supervised temple rebuilding — a family with a multi-generational record of service to the restoration. Binnui (binnûy = 'built up,' a fitting name for a wall-builder) completed the Keilah district's contribution alongside Hashabiah.
19: wayyaḥᵉzeq ʿal-yāḏô ʿezer ben-yêšûaʿ śar hammiṣpāh middāh šēnît: 'and next to him Ezer son of Jeshua, ruler of Mizpah, repaired a second section.' middāh šēnît (second section/measured portion) — middāh (measure, portion) is the chapter's technical term for a work assignment. The section extended from the angle (hammiqṣôaʿ) to the door of the armory (pᵉtaḥ bêt haggibôr), i.e., the military garrison's entry — a key defensive installation.
20: ʾaḥărāyw ḥāzaq bārûḵ ben-zabbay middāh šēnît minhammiqṣôaʿ ʿad-pᵉtaḥ bêt-ʾelyāšîḇ hakkōhēn haggāḏôl: 'after him Baruch son of Zabbai repaired a second measured section, from the angle to the door of the house of Eliashib the high priest.' The phrase ḥeḥĕrāh (he repaired zealously / with ardor) is unique — only Baruch is described with this intensifier. The root ḥārāh usually means to be angry or to burn; here it likely means 'with fervor' or 'zealously.' Baruch's exceptional devotion is singled out as worthy of note within a chapter of devoted workers.
21: ʾaḥărāyw ḥāzaq mᵉrēmôt ben-ʾûriyyāh ben-haqqôṣ middāh šēnît mipᵉtaḥ bêt-ʾelyāšîḇ wᵉʿad-tōsep̄ bêt-ʾelyāšîḇ: 'after him Meremoth son of Uriah son of Hakkoz repaired a second section, from the door of Eliashib's house to the end of Eliashib's house.' Meremoth is the only worker who explicitly appears twice in the chapter on separate sections (vv.4 and 21). His exceptional contribution is consistent with his role in Ezra 8:33 as the priest responsible for weighing and recording the temple treasures — a man of established trustworthiness and initiative.
22: wᵉʾaḥărāyw ḥāzᵉqû hakkōhănîm ʾanšê hakkikkār: 'and after him the priests, the men of the surrounding region, repaired.' Hakkikkār is the plain/circuit around Jerusalem — the valley district. The priests built the section of wall running past their own houses, maintaining the pattern of personal stake driving personal labor. Their participation alongside lay workers, Levites, merchants, and artisans reflects Nehemiah's ability to unite the entire social structure behind the common project.
23: ʾaḥărāyw ḥāzaq binyāmîn wᵉḥaššûḇ negeḏ bêtām: 'after him Benjamin and Hasshub repaired, opposite their house.' The negeḏ bêtô / bêtām (opposite his/their house) formula returns — the household-assignment principle documented throughout the chapter. ʾaḥărāyw ḥāzaq ʿăzaryāh ben-maʿăśêyāh ben-ʿănanyāh ʾēṣel bêtô: Azariah son of Maaseiah son of Ananiah repaired next to his house. ʾēṣel (beside) rather than the usual negeḏ (opposite) — a slight variation suggesting his section was adjacent to rather than facing his residence.
24: ʾaḥărāyw ḥāzaq binnûy ben-hênaḏāḏ middāh šēnît mibêt ʿăzaryāh ʿad-hammiqṣôaʿ wᵉʿad-hapinnāh: 'after him Binnui son of Henadad repaired a second section, from Azariah's house to the angle and to the corner.' Binnui (also in v.18 as a district ruler) appears again here — whether this is the same person building a second assignment or a different Binnui in the Henadad family is debated. The landmarks — the angle (hammiqṣôaʿ) and the corner (hapinnāh) — were architectural features of the wall circuit used as reference points in the official building record.
25: pālāl ben-ʿûzay minnegeḏ hammiqṣôaʿ wᵉhammigdāl hayyôṣēʾ mibêt hammelek hāʿelyônāh: 'Palal son of Uzai repaired opposite the angle and the tower projecting from the upper house of the king.' The upper royal palace (bêt hammelek hāʿelyônāh) was the administrative structure on the southeastern hill; the projecting tower (hammigdāl hayyôṣēʾ) was a defensive fortification protecting the palace approach. Peḏāyāh ben-parʿōš built the adjacent section from the court of the guard (ḥăṣar hammmaṭṭārāh), which was a known Jerusalem landmark (Jer 32:2, 8, 12; 33:1; 37:21; 38:6, 13, 28).
26: wᵉhannᵉtînîm hayyôšᵉḇîm bāʿōp̄el ʿad-negeḏ šaʿar hammayim: 'and the temple servants living on the Ophel repaired up to opposite the Water Gate.' The Nethinim (nᵉtînîm = 'given ones,' those dedicated to temple service — possibly descendants of Solomon's Canaanite laborers or prisoners of war assigned to temple duties, Ezra 8:20) lived on the Ophel, the hill between the City of David and the temple mount. Their participation in wall-building continued beyond their assigned section: wᵉhammigdāl hayyôṣēʾ, the projecting tower. The Water Gate (šaʿar hammayim) led to the Gihon spring; it becomes important in Neh 8:1, 3, 16 as the assembly point for Ezra's public Torah reading.
27: ʾaḥărāyw ḥāzᵉqû hattᵉqôʿîm middāh šēnît minnegeḏ hammigdāl haggāḏôl hayyôṣēʾ wᵉʿad ḥômat hāʿōp̄el: 'after them the Tekoites repaired a second section, from opposite the great projecting tower to the wall of the Ophel.' The Tekoites reappear — despite their nobles' refusal to serve (v.5), the common workers built not only their original section but also a second one. The contrast is pointed: the nobles who refused to bend their necks are permanently recorded as shirkers; the workers who doubled their effort are recorded as faithful. The community saw and remembered both.
28: mēʿal šaʿar hassûsîm ḥāzᵉqû hakkōhănîm: 'above the Horse Gate the priests repaired.' The Horse Gate (šaʿar hassûsîm) was on the eastern wall of the temple mount, providing access to the royal stables — each priest repaired the section opposite his own house (negeḏ bêtô). This is the second mention of priests building opposite their own houses (cf. v.22), reflecting that priestly residences were distributed along the eastern wall adjacent to the temple complex.
29: ʾaḥărāyw ḥāzaq ṣāḏôq ben-ʾimmēr negeḏ bêtô: 'after him Zadok son of Immer repaired, opposite his own house.' ʾaḥărāyw ḥāzaq šᵉmayāh ben-šeḵanyāh šōmēr šaʿar hammizrāḥ: 'after him Shemaiah son of Shecaniah, keeper/guardian of the East Gate, repaired.' šōmēr šaʿar (gatekeeper) was a formal Levitical office (1 Chr 9:17-27); the gatekeeper repaired the very gate he was responsible for guarding — a fitting union of function and labor.
30: ʾaḥărāyw ḥāzaq ḥananeyāh ben-šelemyāh waḥānûn ben-ṣālāp̄ haššišší: 'after him Hananiah son of Shelemiah and Hanun son of Zalaph the sixth repaired.' haššišší (the sixth) designates Zalaph's birth order in the family — an unusually specific genealogical marker that suggests Hanun is identified by family position to distinguish him from other men named Hanun. ʾaḥărāyw ḥizzᵉq mešullām ben-bᵉreḵyāh negeḏ niškātô: Meshullam son of Berechiah repaired his second section opposite his chamber (niškāh). Niškāh (storage room, cell) indicates Meshullam had a room within the temple precincts — a common arrangement for priests and officials.
31: ʾaḥărāyw ḥāzaq malkiyyāh ben-haṣṣōrᵉp̄î ʿad-bêt hannᵉtînîm wᵉhārōkᵉlîm negeḏ šaʿar hammipqāḏ wᵉʿad ʿăliyyat happinnāh: 'after him Malchijah the son of the goldsmith repaired as far as the house of the temple servants and the merchants, opposite the Muster Gate and as far as the upper room of the corner.' The Muster Gate (šaʿar hammipqāḏ, also 'Inspection Gate' from pāqaḏ = to number/muster/appoint) was the gate through which the troops were reviewed. The goldsmiths' and merchants' quarter near the temple reflects the commercial activity that naturally clustered near the sacrificial and pilgrimage center.
32: ûḇên ʿăliyyat happinnāh lᵉšaʿar haṣṣōʾn ḥāzᵉqû haṣṣōrᵉp̄îm wᵉhārōkᵉlîm: 'and between the upper room of the corner and the Sheep Gate the goldsmiths and the merchants repaired.' The chapter ends where it began — the Sheep Gate in v.1. The circuit is complete. The goldsmiths and merchants built the final stretch, closing the ring. The chapter as a whole is a theological document disguised as a building record: 41 named individuals and groups, priests and servants, rulers and artisans, men and women, locals and visitors — all united in the single task of reconstituting the city's defenses under YHWH's good hand.
